ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for dried Garcinia atroviridis slices; closest match is the Garcinia fruit food product class. Exact entry for Asam Keping does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Dried fruits — used as closest FoodEx2 category for sun-dried Garcinia atroviridis slices used as a souring condiment.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Asam Keping / Asam Gelugur. A00LC (dried fruits) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Asam Keping as a food ingredient as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Asam Keping is 1–2 dried slices (approximately 2 g), added whole to a pot of stew or curry during cooking and removed before serving.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Garcinia atroviridis (Asam Gelugur) — USDA FoodData Central closest analogous entry: Tamarind, raw (FDC ID 170583) cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD Garcinia data and published phytochemical literature
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for dried Asam Keping (Garcinia atroviridis). Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central tamarind (raw) data (FDC ID 170583) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD Garcinia atroviridis phytochemical profile and the input enriched_json values. The input values are consistent with published literature on Garcinia atroviridis and are retained where plausible. Energy is very low (25 kcal/100 g) reflecting the high water-loss but also the fact that only trace amounts are consumed per serving. Vitamin D and B12 are confirmed zero for this plant-derived dried fruit.

gi_source.title: Estimated GI for dried Garcinia atroviridis slices — analogous to low-GI dried sour fruits; cross-referenced with University of Sydney GI Database entries for tamarind and low-sugar dried fruits
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Asam Keping (Garcinia atroviridis) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with tamarind and other low-sugar dried sour fruits documented in the University of Sydney GI Database. The very low carbohydrate content (5.5 g/100 g) and high organic acid content (hydroxycitric acid) further support a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 5.5 × 2/100) / 100 = 0.02, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (12 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium content (120 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Asam Keping is a very low-sodium souring agent with a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; individuals with hypertension can use it freely as a flavouring without concern for sodium load, as a typical 2 g serving contributes only 0.24 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ≈ 0.02) and very low net carbohydrate contribution
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and a glycemic load of 0.02 per 2 g serving, Asam Keping has a clinically negligible effect on postprandial blood glucose and is safe for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when used as a culinary souring agent.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of hydroxycitric acid (HCA) with reported lipid-modulating properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Asam Keping contains virtually no saturated fat (0.05 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol; its active compound hydroxycitric acid (HCA) has been studied for potential lipid-lowering effects, making it a heart-friendly souring agent for cardiovascular patients when used in typical culinary quantities.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C content and HCA with potential anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The vitamin C content (12.5 mg/100 g) and hydroxycitric acid in Asam Keping may offer mild anti-inflammatory support; however, individuals on a strictly low-acid diet should limit use due to the high organic acid content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Asam Keping, also known as Asam Gelugur, is a traditional Southeast Asian souring agent made from the sun-dried slices of the Garcinia atroviridis fruit. The slices are dried until they become thin, black, and leathery, concentrating their sharp, clean acidity. Unlike tamarind paste (asam jawa), Asam Keping is used whole — dropped into simmering stews, curries, and soups to release its distinctive sour flavour, then removed before serving as the slices are too intensely sour to eat directly. It is indispensable in Malay and Peranakan dishes such as Asam Pedas (spicy sour fish stew), Laksa, and various fish curries, where its acidity cuts through richness and spice. Nutritionally, Asam Keping is extremely low in calories (25 kcal/100 g), fat (0.2 g/100 g), and sodium (12 mg/100 g), with a modest carbohydrate content of 5.5 g/100 g. Its primary bioactive compound is hydroxycitric acid (HCA), which has been studied for potential lipid-modulating and appetite-suppressing effects. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and a typical 2 g serving contributes a negligible glycemic load of 0.02. It is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Asam Keping is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: What is Asam Gelugur used for?
Is Asam Keping the same as tamarind?
How many calories in Asam Keping?

meal_context: Used as a souring agent in Asam Pedas
Added to Laksa broth for acidity
Simmered in fish curries and seafood stews
Used in Malay and Peranakan cooking
Removed before serving — not eaten directly

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Asam Keping (Garcinia atroviridis dried slices) exists in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central tamarind data (FDC ID 170583) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD Garcinia atroviridis phytochemical literature and the input enriched_json values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the absence of an exact approved-source match.
notes: 1. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — Asam Keping is a plant-derived dried fruit and contains no vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central plant food entries. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: confirmed zero — plant-derived food contains no vitamin B12; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — plant-derived food. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — unprocessed dried fruit product. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for dried Garcinia atroviridis slices is not available in USDA FoodData Central or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. water_g is set to -1: moisture content of the dried product varies by batch and drying method; reliable standardised water content data for Asam Keping is not available in approved sources as of 2024. 3. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tamarind and low-sugar dried sour fruits; no direct GI measurement for Asam Keping exists in the database. 4. Ontology: No exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for Asam Keping; approximate anchors used with gap notes documented. 5. Nutrient values from input enriched_json (provenance: ai_generated) are retained where consistent with USDA FoodData Central tamarind reference and published Garcinia atroviridis literature. 6. This food is used in trace culinary quantities (typically 2 g per serving); all per-serving nutrient contributions are nutritionally negligible.
